children.text: Overall orientation
Mandates and background

12.1	The United Nations Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD) is responsible for assisting developing countries in integrating beneficially into the global economy in support of inclusive, resilient and sustainable growth and development. This task is more critical than ever, in light of the profound transformations, climate change and environmental challenges, geopolitical tensions, growing conflict, increasing macroeconomic challenges and growing systemic risks. In the Geneva Consensus, the outcome document of the sixteenth quadrennial session of UNCTAD, the member States of UNCTAD reaffirmed that international trade remains a powerful means for economic growth, structural transformation and the achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals, but that developing countries with limited productive capacities and trade infrastructure have challenges integrating into regional and global value and supply chains. They also emphasized the need for multilateral cooperation and multilateralism to boost sustainable development and the building of an open and prosperous global economy. In the Geneva Consensus, UNCTAD is requested to continue to enhance the ability of countries to build a fairer, more equitable, resilient, inclusive, just and sustainable world, while giving priority consideration to the needs and interests of developing countries. Therefore, UNCTAD will support least developed countries, landlocked and transit developing countries, small island developing States, African countries, other structurally weak and vulnerable and small economies, net-food-importing developing countries and countries in conflict and post-conflict situations, while also taking account of the challenges facing middle-income countries. Moreover, UNCTAD remains the focal point within the United Nations system for the integrated treatment of trade and development and interrelated issues in the areas of finance, technology, investment and sustainable development, and it will also contribute to supporting implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.
Strategy and external factors for 2027

12.2	The global economy continues to face subdued economic growth, with persistent inequalities within and among countries. Economies, particularly the most vulnerable, are affected by compounding challenges, including uncertainty related to trade, limited fiscal space, weak investment levels, high and unsustainable debt burdens, digital divides and fragile productive capacities. Therefore, fostering more inclusive and sustainable development and enhancing resilience to these challenges will continue to guide the work of UNCTAD, in accordance with the particular needs and challenges of the member States and the priority areas set out in the Geneva Consensus. In line with its mandate, UNCTAD will continue to rely on its three interrelated pillars of work: (a) conducting high-quality and evidence-based research and policy analysis that contribute to national, regional and international policies with the aim of generating inclusive and sustainable development under the principle of leaving no one behind; (b) providing technical cooperation to developing countries on the basis of such analysis; and (c) bringing member States together through its intergovernmental machinery to build consensus on policies that allow developing countries to maximize the opportunities of globalization and economic integration, as well as to address cross-cutting economic, social and environmental challenges.

12.3	UNCTAD will remain committed to addressing interrelated issues essential to all countries in attaining sustainable and equitable growth and development, while being particularly mindful of the needs and priorities of developing countries. UNCTAD will also support developing countries with work on South-South and triangular cooperation, as a complement to and not a substitute for North-South cooperation, and regional integration.

12.4	In accordance with its mandate in the interrelated areas of trade and development, and as the custodian of nine Sustainable Development Goal indicators at the global level, UNCTAD will support the implementation, monitoring and review of the 2030 Agenda and the relevant targets of the Goals, including by continuing to develop international standards and recommending methodologies for monitoring Goal indicators. It will also support the implementation of the Pact for the Future, notably the reform of the international financial architecture, the work on beyond gross domestic product (GDP), interoperable data governance and making the digital economy more inclusive. UNCTAD will also support borrower countries in addressing their debt challenges, exchanging experiences and fostering cooperation to improve their debt management practices, in line with the Sevilla Commitment.

12.5	Alongside global agendas, UNCTAD will ensure the implementation of mandates reconfirmed and reinforced at its sixteenth quadrennial session, as outlined in the Geneva Consensus. In particular, UNCTAD will further strengthen its support for the least developed countries through dedicated graduation support; for landlocked and transit developing countries, by supporting the implementation of the Awaza Programme of Action for Landlocked Developing Countries for the Decade 2024–2034; and for small island developing States, by supporting the implementation of the Antigua and Barbuda Agenda for Small Island Developing States: A Renewed Declaration for Resilient Prosperity, as well as by building on the UNCTAD Strategy to support Small Island Developing States. Moreover, UNCTAD will enhance the capacity of developing countries to participate in e-commerce.

12.6	To address the adverse effects of climate change and environmental degradation, which can hinder economic growth, UNCTAD will continue to contribute to new global trade, financial and policy frameworks that more fully integrate inclusiveness, climate change and the environment. This work includes supporting the scaling up of climate finance, using the potential of trade for climate action, reducing greenhouse gas emissions of the logistics of trade and global value chains, boosting sustainable investments and supporting a transition to a low-emission sustainable economy, including through critical minerals needed for renewable energy. UNCTAD will advance the issue of the integrated treatment of development economics in international forums, such as the annual sessions of the Conference of the Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change or the Conference of the Parties to the Convention on Biological Diversity.

12.7	To support its member States in promoting structural transformation, UNCTAD will continue to support developing countries in making more accurate diagnostics, inform policy choices by measuring productive capacities and examine the growing significance of services as a new frontier for structural transformation. UNCTAD will also remain at the forefront of understanding the impact of technological advancements on structural transformation and continue its work to make digitalization a force of inclusivity, including those advancements created by emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence. Given the potential of regional economic integration for structural transformation, UNCTAD will continue to support regional and interregional integration processes, such as the implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area or the Global System of Trade Preferences.

12.8	UNCTAD will continue to provide timely and high-quality statistical information and indicators in the interrelated areas of trade and development through its Statistics, Data and Digital Service. It will continue to report on developments relating to the Sustainable Development Goals by providing an update on the evolution of a selection of official indicators and on progress in the development of new concepts and methodologies, including analytical and statistical support in collaboration with other relevant international organizations on redefining development metrics of progress beyond GDP. UNCTAD will strengthen synergies between statistics and information technology to enhance its capacity in data science, artificial intelligence and digital transformation. It will foster a culture of innovation and leverage the advances in digital and emerging technologies to enhance its knowledge products and provide data-driven tools to address development challenges. Furthermore, UNCTAD will support developing countries in building their capacity to collect and compile data and measure progress in sustainable, inclusive and equitable trade, growth and development. It will use data collected and reported by countries of the South within their own frameworks to measure South-South cooperation and inform global debates on development support.

12.9	UNCTAD will continue to support countries, through technical cooperation, in building their resilience to economic shocks. It will support countries in building productive capacities related to financing for development, investment, international trade, science and technology and logistics.

12.10	With regard to cooperation with other entities at the global, regional, national and local levels, UNCTAD will continue to combine its expertise with the reach and in-country presence of partners. Regionally, UNCTAD will work closely with partner organizations, such as the secretariat of the African Continental Free Trade Area through the UNCTAD Regional Office for Africa, as well as regional economic and business communities and strategic development partners, to foster regional economic integration and growth. UNCTAD will continue to foster cooperation with international financial institutions, multilateral development banks and other relevant intergovernmental and multilateral forums to contribute to international policy debates on trade, investment and finance.

12.11	With regard to inter-agency coordination and liaison, UNCTAD will continue to collaborate with resident coordinator offices, including through common country assessments and United Nations Sustainable Development Cooperation Frameworks. It will collaborate with the regional commissions through joint projects and initiatives in areas of trade facilitation, sustainable development and the integration of value chains. UNCTAD will continue to work with the World Trade Organization (WTO) and the International Trade Centre (ITC) in a complementary manner to support developing countries in integrating into and benefiting from the multilateral trading system. UNCTAD will continue to contribute to the Inter-Agency Task Force on Financing for Development and collaborate with relevant United Nations entities in the system to implement the Sevilla Commitment, including co-chairing the working group with the Bretton Woods institutions on the Principles on Promoting Responsible Sovereign Borrowing and Lending, and proposals for their implementation. UNCTAD will support the Inter-Agency and Expert Group on Sustainable Development Goal Indicators and lead the United Nations Inter-Agency Cluster on Trade and Productive Capacity in “delivering as one” operational activities at the country level. UNCTAD will support the implementation of outcomes related to science, technology and innovation of the 2030 Agenda and the Pact for the Future, including the multi-stakeholder working group on data governance. To implement the Pact for the Future, UNCTAD will collaborate with the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) on Beyond GDP, in support of the Secretary-General’s High-level Expert Group on Beyond GDP, and the reform of the international financial architecture, with the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime and the Department of Economic and Social Affairs on illicit financial flows, and with the Statistical Commission of the United Nations on South-South cooperation. In coordination with the United Nations Environment Programme, UNDP and the Climate Action Team, UNCTAD will continue to lead the United Nations Task Force on Critical Energy Transition Minerals.
12.12	With regard to the external factors, the overall plan for 2027 is based on the following planning assumptions:

(a)	Up-to-date and accurate economic and financial information and data at the country and regional levels continue to be available and cooperation continues with relevant entities on the production of statistics;
(b)	Extrabudgetary funding continues to be available for technical cooperation programmes, and conditions and capacities exist in member States to adopt and implement policies and strategies;
(c)	There is political will to achieve consensus in intergovernmental meetings on matters related to UNCTAD;
(d)	Member States will provide the necessary support for the implementation of mandated activities at the global, regional and national levels.

12.13	UNCTAD will continue to mainstream the cross-cutting issue of gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls in its operational activities, deliverables and results. UNCTAD will strengthen the capacity of member States to design and implement policies and establish institutions, frameworks and/or mechanisms that support women’s economic empowerment, economic security and rights and enhance their economic and digital skills and opportunities. UNCTAD will continue to work on the links between gender equality, women’s and girls’ empowerment and trade and development through its women-focused programmes, and advocate for gender-sensitive policy responses. UNCTAD will continue to analyse the barriers that prevent women from fully participating in and benefiting from trade, including as entrepreneurs.

12.14	In line with the United Nations Disability Inclusion Strategy and the Geneva Consensus, UNCTAD will continue its efforts to support disability inclusion in trade or technical cooperation projects. UNCTAD will further advance the work of the Inter-Agency Technical Working Group on Trade and Disability Inclusion established in conjunction with the International Labour Organization, ITC and WTO to integrate and enhance disability inclusion in trade and development agendas. UNCTAD will strive to ensure that all venues and conference facilities are accessible to persons with disabilities.

12.15	UNCTAD will also contribute to member States’ efforts to support the role of youth in sustainable development. UNCTAD will continue to engage with young people through a dedicated network and flagship events designed to integrate the youth perspective into global trade and development policies.
Adjustments arising from the UN80 Initiative

12.16	The proposed programme plan for 2027 reflects changes arising from the UN80 Initiative. In particular, the tables of deliverables reflect updated deliverables for 2026, in line with the approved programme budget for 2026.

12.17	UNCTAD will be guided by any future decisions taken in the context of the UN80 Initiative, in accordance with the applicable rules and procedures, including decisions, resolutions and established practice of the competent intergovernmental organs. This includes, among others, any decisions concerning proposals on strengthening collaboration with the Department of Economic and Social Affairs and the regional commissions through joint mechanisms and proposals to establish joint knowledge hubs. UNCTAD will jointly explore options for strengthening macroeconomic analysis capacities, with a view to contributing more harmonized analysis and policy support to Member States. UNCTAD will also consider proposals aimed at streamlining support for the least developed countries, landlocked developing countries and small island developing States and strengthening and optimizing organizational arrangements in this regard, as appropriate. Further, UNCTAD will explore, in collaboration with the Department of Economic and Social Affairs, the establishment of joint knowledge hubs, co-led by the best-placed entities, to pool knowledge on key issues spread across United Nations development system entities and link to regional and resident coordinator offices.

Evaluation activities
12.20	The following evaluations conducted by UNCTAD and completed in 2025 have guided the proposed programme plan for 2027:
(a)	UNCTAD e-commerce and digital economy programme;
(b)	Three evaluations of projects related to trade, transport and economic development issues.

12.21	In response to the results of the evaluations referenced above, UNCTAD has made efforts to strengthen sustainability planning, improve project management practices, deepen partnerships with resident coordinator offices, enhance the targeted dissemination of research products and continue to mainstream a gender perspective, disability inclusion and environmental considerations in its work. The eTrade Reform Tracker, for example, will sustain outcomes by clarifying responsibilities, enhancing transparency and supporting structured follow-up on e-trade readiness actions.
12.22	The following evaluations to be conducted by UNCTAD are planned for 2027:
(a)	Evaluation of subprogramme 5, Africa, least developed countries and special programmes;
(b)	Four project evaluations.
Programme of work
Subprogramme 1 – Globalization, interdependence and development
Objective

12.23	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance inclusive and sustainable development, sustained growth, full employment and decent work for all through evidence-based economic policies and strategies at the national, regional and international levels and to achieve progress towards durable solutions on debt, economic resilience and poverty eradication in developing countries, especially the least developed countries, including through North-South cooperation, complemented but not substituted by South-South and triangular cooperation.
Strategy

12.24	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will identify specific needs and measures arising from the interdependence of trade, finance, investment, technology and macroeconomic policies, from the point of view of their effects on development, through the three pillars of work of UNCTAD referred to in paragraph 12.2 above. Specifically, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Support developing countries in promoting structural economic transformation through statistics, capacity-building and technical cooperation, identify policy options, design industrial and productive transformation strategies and foster regional and cross-regional partnerships for sustainable growth;

(b)	Support developing countries in the realization of the 2030 Agenda by fostering a more inclusive and resilient global economy and by enhancing the efficiency of the global economic system, international trade and debt sustainability;

(c)	Undertake analytical and technical work on debt issues – fostering debt-management practices, promoting responsible borrowing and lending practices, supporting exchanges among borrower countries on debt challenges, and contributing to global debt data transparency and harmonization;
(d)	Support countries in identifying policy options to scale up domestic resources and strengthen international resource mobilization;

(e)	Support countries in engaging in discussions on reforming the international financial architecture, multilateral development banks and credit rating agencies, by providing them with relevant analyses and information;

(f)	Conduct analytical work on assessing the role of official development assistance as a key component of financing for development, including in mobilizing additional public and private finance and the role of such assistance in international net financial flows, both North-South and South-North;

(g)	Undertake research and analysis on the trends of the global economy and prospects for closer cooperation and integration among developing countries to inform their development strategies, including by promoting dialogue and best practice among existing regionally integrated structures;

(h)	Provide technical assistance and research and analysis to support the Palestinian people, in line with paragraphs 80.68 and 80.69 of the Geneva Consensus and responding to the request of the General Assembly in its resolutions 77/22 on the Committee on the Exercise of the Inalienable Rights of the Palestinian People and 79/141 on assistance to the Palestinian people;

(i)	Conduct analytical work on climate finance and sustainable development to assist developing countries in leveraging financial resources to address vulnerabilities, with the aim of building productive capacities resilient to extreme weather events exacerbated by climate change.
12.25	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Increased coherence between international economic rules, practices and processes and national policies and development strategies;
(b)	Improved mobilization of domestic financial resources as well as international resources, including through South-South cooperation;
(c)	More effective debt management and a shared understanding at the international level of debt issues and debt sustainability, and facilitated exchanges among borrowing countries;
(d)	Increased understanding by developing countries of the global economic environment and of policy choices for inclusive and sustained development;
(e)	Trade and development policies that are responsive to the challenges posed by climate change and the type of support and action needed.
Programme performance in 2025
Trade and Development Foresights informs global policy discussions and responses

12.26	In 2025, to adapt to the rapidly changing global economic environment and to better align the subprogramme’s research with the needs of policymakers in developing countries, the subprogramme released a newly restructured Trade and Development Foresights 2025, which highlighted emerging risks – especially for countries that are highly vulnerable to external shocks – and outlined policy priorities to strengthen resilience in a global economy facing persistent challenges. The analysis equipped policymakers and informed international policy discussions, contributing to financial policy debates in global policy forums and elevating the perspectives of developing countries.

children.text: Result 3: borrower countries manage debt in a more efficient and sustainable way – Proposed programme plan for 2027

12.32	Today, approximately 3.4 billion people live in countries that spend more on servicing their public debt than on either health or education. The Debt Management and Financial Analysis System, aimed at helping Member States to sustainably manage their debt, is currently used in over 60 countries. To further increase debt management capacity, UNCTAD has been convening meetings to support borrowing countries since 2023.
Lessons learned and planned change

12.33	The lesson for the subprogramme was that there was an increased need to support countries using the Debt Management and Financial Analysis System, particularly through new functionalities, in order to respond to the evolving nature of debt markets and the rising significance of private creditors relative to official creditors, which has added new transparency and data-quality requirements for borrower countries. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will help countries to adopt a new version of the Debt Management and Financial Analysis System, tailored to meet the additional requirements, and will focus on providing a permanent platform for coordination and knowledge-sharing. Furthermore, the subprogramme will support the establishment of the borrowers’ platform mandated by the Sevilla Commitment to help to increase cooperation, share experiences, build capacities and coordinate approaches to managing debt.

Subprogramme 2 – Investment and enterprise
Objective

12.36	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to advance inclusive growth and sustainable development through investment and enterprise development for productive capacity-building, economic diversification and job creation.
Strategy
12.37	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Assist member States through research, policy analysis and technical assistance in designing and implementing policies to scale up mobilization of public and private finance and enhanced investment and entrepreneurship for sustainable development;

(b)	Monitor, assess and analyse regional and global trends and prospects in international investment and prepare the annual World Investment Report and other analytical publications on international investment for development;

(c)	Promote policy frameworks that create an enabling environment at all levels in support of developing countries to better attract, increase and facilitate investments for the achievement of sustainable development;

(d)	Conduct reviews of national investment policies and backstop policy dialogue on the international investment regime and support and design new international investment agreements that are better aligned with nationally and internationally agreed development objectives;

(e)	Provide policy advice to Governments on investment promotion, including on incentives in the context of the global tax reform implications for developing countries, and support with the implementation of business practices and investment facilitation for development agreements, including through digital government tools;

(f)	Promote innovative financing for the Sustainable Development Goals, including through stock exchanges and institutional funds, as well as incentives in the context of the global tax reform implications for developing countries;

(g)	Support enhancement of the international competitiveness of member States’ enterprises through advice on policies aimed at stimulating enterprise development, including mobilizing global efforts in financing and investment in micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises to support their sustainable development transformation, as well as by promoting best practices in corporate social responsibility, accounting and sustainability reporting;
(h)	Foster dialogue and an exchange of best practices related to investment and enterprise development issues through consensus-building mechanisms and the World Investment Forum;

(i)	Provide an assessment of the impact of multiple and cascading challenges on investment, global value chains and enterprise development, as well as policy advice, frameworks and tools for recovery.
12.38	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Increased capacity of member States to close the gaps related to Sustainable Development Goal investment, especially with regard to climate change mitigation and adaptation, in particular for countries that are most vulnerable to the adverse impacts of climate change;

(b)	Improved ability of countries to leverage policies, innovations and tools, and to address key and emerging issues related to investment and investment policies that promote development, including policies on international investment agreements and on the opportunities of remittances and diaspora investment for development;
(c)	Member States collecting, analysing and reporting on foreign direct investment and multinational enterprise data and formulating development-oriented investment policies;
(d)	Additional resources leveraged and channelled towards global investment development objectives and addressing concerns pertaining to “SDG washing” and “greenwashing”;
(e)	Increased productive capacity, including through attracting investment capital, growing business linkages with multinational enterprises and participating in global and regional value chains;
(f)	Mitigation of the effect of multiple and cascading challenges on the ability of member States to attract and benefit from investment for development and to foster private sector recovery;
(g)	Progress by member States towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 1, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 12, 13, 16 and 17.
Programme performance in 2025
Strengthened entrepreneurship ecosystems for inclusive and innovative enterprise development

12.39	Entrepreneurship can be a driver of productive transformation, yet structural constraints often hinder micro- and small enterprises in many developing countries from growing, innovating and integrating into national, regional and global value chains. To help to build more dynamic and sustainable entrepreneurship ecosystems for inclusive and innovative enterprise development, the subprogramme has strengthened its policy guidance, capacity-building and advisory support for member States. This includes the provision of entrepreneurship-related recommendations from investment policy reviews, targeted analysis and advisory services delivered through the Entrepreneurship Policy Framework, and the expansion of the UNCTAD Entrepreneurship Development Programme (EMPRETEC) to a growing number of beneficiary countries to reinforce entrepreneurial skills and enterprise support systems.

Result 1: accelerated Sustainable Development Goal investment – Programme performance in 2025 and target for 2027

12.41	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 762 investment-development stakeholders adopting recommendations from the set of priority actions identified by UNCTAD to accelerate investment in the Sustainable Development Goals, such as the reorientation of investment promotion strategies, the establishment of global partnerships for sustainable investment, the formulation of a new generation of international investment treaties and guarantees, enhanced regional and South-South investment partnerships, innovative financing mechanisms and the reorientation of financial markets, which exceeded the planned target of 650 investment-development stakeholders.

12.43	The subprogramme’s work contributed to 206 UNCTAD instruments being adopted by member States in support of a better business environment, including in the promotion and facilitation of investment for development, the adoption of digital tools to facilitate the provision of investment-related information, and the streamlining of administrative processes and procedures related to business, which exceeded the planned target of 203 instruments.

Result 3: increased adoption of sustainable investment practices – Proposed programme plan for 2027

12.45	Sustainable investment practices help to align global capital flows with the Sustainable Development Goals, yet many markets face challenges related to transparency, comparability and the effective implementation of sustainability standards. To support countries in strengthening the sustainable development impact of investment flows, the subprogramme has expanded its policy guidance, analytical tools and multi-stakeholder platforms aimed at improving sustainability practices in capital markets, such as the Sustainable Stock Exchanges Initiative, the Global Sustainable Finance Observatory and regional partnerships for the promotion of sustainability reporting.
Lessons learned and planned change

12.46	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of strengthening UNCTAD support for effective de-risking strategies to help channel investment and finance towards the Goals, particularly in developing countries where perceived and real risks can often constrain capital flows. In applying this lesson, the subprogramme will place emphasis on de-risking support by promoting transparency, strengthening sustainability reporting, supporting coherent regulatory approaches and helping countries to adopt practices that reduce information asymmetries and lower investor uncertainty. To do so, the subprogramme will leverage and integrate the tools and platforms it has developed, while also enhancing its broader support to countries on risk-sharing instruments and blended finance.

Subprogramme 3 – International trade and commodities
Objective

12.49	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to ensure that international trade and commodities enable the economic diversification, sustainable and resilient economy and multilateralism transformations needed to create a more inclusive, resilient, sustainable and prosperous world through the participation of all member States.
Strategy
12.50	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:
(a)	Support developing countries in fostering structural transformation through economic diversification towards a more sustainable and resilient economy;

(b)	Enhance the participation of developing countries in the multilateral and regional trading systems, ensuring that developing countries’ participation in trade in goods and services at all levels of the value chain, including through broader UNCTAD support for the analysis and utilization of the relevant data, works as a driver for inclusive and sustainable development;
(c)	Support the adoption of, improvement in and enforcement of national and regional competition and consumer protection legislation, through best practices, guidelines and peer reviews;

(d)	Strengthen the capacity of trade policymakers to make well-informed policy decisions through improved understanding of and transparency in international trade and trade policy trends and trade-related regulations, and build private sector capacities to cope with policy and market requirements;

(e)	Foster the mainstreaming of gender equality and disability inclusion into trade policies and build the capacity of member States through research findings, training workshops and policy dialogues to design and implement trade policies that allow women and persons with disabilities to benefit more from the opportunities arising from international trade;

(f)	Contribute to research on international trade as an engine for development in the report of the Inter-Agency Task Force on Financing for Development, The Sustainable Development Goals Report, World Tariff Profiles and World Economic Situation and Prospects;

(g)	Bearing in mind the current challenging global context, as described in paragraphs 12, 13, 18 and 19 of the Geneva Consensus, monitor and report on current trade measures, trends and policies and their impact on inclusive, sustainable and resilient development to provide policy recommendations to member States;

(h)	Examine the effects of climate change in terms of trade on developing countries, and how environmental sustainability can promote global trade and inclusive development, including through trade policies facilitating global energy transition and supporting the competitiveness of developing countries in the production and trade of renewable and low-emission energy;

(i)	Support member States, through research, capacity-building activities and the fostering of policy dialogue, in their efforts to break away from commodity dependence and realize trade and economic diversification gains, including for critical minerals, by adding value to their commodities and strengthening non-commodity activities.
12.51	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:

(a)	Enhanced understanding and policy response regarding current trends in the global economy, including ongoing environmental degradation, and improved ability of member States to benefit from international trade and foster the recovery of the trade sectors;
(b)	Member States designing and implementing trade-policy responses to climate change from a development perspective;
(c)	Member States identifying and addressing trade barriers and supply-side obstacles that disproportionately affect women and girls, and persons with disabilities;

(d)	Member States and the international community understanding and addressing the cost and market access impacts of non-tariff measures, as well as their beneficial use for inclusive and sustainable development policies;
(e)	Member States achieving positive development outcomes in relation to increased trade integration and activity;
(f)	Member States seizing opportunities emerging from commodity trade and enhanced international and regional cooperation;
(g)	Member States’ increased adoption, revision and/or effective implementation of competition and consumer protection legislation and institutional frameworks;

(h)	Member States harnessing opportunities related to trade in environmentally sustainable products, and increasing capacities to assess the economic potential of the ocean, biodiversity and plastic substitutes-related sectors, and devising integrated sustainable use and trade action plans;
(i)	Member States creating an enabling environment for the promotion of services and the creative economy to leapfrog into new, high-growth sectors of the world economy;
(j)	Progress by member States towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 12, 13, 14 and 17.
Programme performance in 2025
Enhanced consumer product safety

12.52	UNCTAD data indicate that approximately 40 per cent of member States have reported having product safety legislation, highlighting the potential for strengthening regulatory frameworks and fostering global alignment through increased coherence, comparability and mutual recognition of product safety regimes. As the focal point for consumer protection within the United Nations system, the subprogramme promotes the implementation of the United Nations guidelines for consumer protection by undertaking analysis, supporting countries in designing and implementing effective consumer policies and providing a forum for intergovernmental deliberations. To improve consumer product safety globally, and driven by the subprogramme’s long-standing work and consensus-building under its Intergovernmental Group of Experts on Consumer Protection Law and Policy, member States adopted the United Nations principles for consumer product safety at the Ninth United Nations Conference to Review All Aspects of the Set of Multilaterally Agreed Equitable Principles and Rules for the Control of Restrictive Business Practices, held in July 2025. These principles provide the first globally agreed blueprint for modern product safety frameworks.

children.text: Result 3: timely non-tariff measures data for better regulatory practices and economic development – Proposed programme plan for 2027

12.58	Non-tariff measures can affect international trade by increasing compliance costs, making it more expensive for businesses, and can create significant obstacles to trade. Non-tariff measures are inherently complex and encompass a wide range of regulatory measures, including product requirements, health and safety regulations and import quotas, whose trade effects are not easily measurable. Due to this complexity and diversity, the timely availability of data on such measures can help to avoid high compliance costs for member States. The subprogramme has been leading the effort for global transparency in non-tariff measures by collecting and disseminating related data systematically, enabling a better understanding by policymakers and traders, especially from developing countries.
Lessons learned and planned change

12.59	The lesson for the subprogramme was the importance of improving the accessibility and timeliness of data related to non-tariff measures to facilitate analysis by policymakers, especially from developing countries. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will leverage and use artificial intelligence to collect and support more frequent updates of the non-tariff measures data in the Trade Analysis and Information System (TRAINS) database, and will enhance the capacity and knowledge of member States to use these data to support their decision-making and regulatory processes through tailored capacity-building.

Subprogramme 4 – Technology and logistics
Objective

12.62	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to harness innovation and technology, including e-commerce and the digital economy, improve trade logistics and increase human capacities for inclusive and sustainable trade and development in developing countries and economies in transition.
Strategy
12.63	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Provide research and analysis and policy recommendations for harnessing the digital economy and narrowing digital and data divides, including on the challenges of digital and emerging technology such as artificial intelligence, for supporting an enabling environment for digital infrastructure development, for improving digital literacy and skills and for supporting the participation of micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises and women- and youth-led business in the digital economy, and support developing countries in measuring e-commerce and the digital economy;

(b)	Foster and ensure that the interests of developing countries are identified and integrated into international policy dialogues on e-commerce and the digital economy, including through the Intergovernmental Group of Experts on E-commerce and the Digital Economy and UNCTAD in synergy with the United Nations system and other organizations, and contribute to initiatives, debates and events on digital trade and digital economy from a development perspective, to the implementation of the World Summit on the Information Society architecture and to the implementation of the relevant commitments of the Global Digital Compact;

(c)	Support developing countries in building their readiness to harness gains from electronic commerce and digital trade, including through eTrade Readiness Assessments and follow-up on the implementation of their policy recommendations, as well as through digital trade negotiation support;

(d)	Support the development of an enabling environment at all levels for developing countries to mobilize resources through trade and investment, address challenges to their public revenue generation capacity and build circular approaches to the digital economy, to support their digital transformation and the availability of digital public goods, infrastructure and services;

(e)	Ensure that international debates on science, technology and innovation incorporate the perspectives and priorities of developing countries through the Commission on Science and Technology for Development and the Technology Facilitation Mechanism;

(f)	Inform policies and deliberations on science, technology and innovation for development, especially on frontier technologies, and contribute to the implementation of the World Summit on the Information Society architecture and the relevant commitments of the Global Digital Compact, including by servicing the work of the Working Group on Data Governance of the Commission on Science and Technology for Development;

(g)	Foster international cooperation in science, technology and innovation, including through the Commission on Science and Technology for Development, to support national action through capacity-building and effective policy frameworks, and continue to support member States, especially developing countries, in collaboration with other relevant institutions, to enhance their capacity for innovation, research, development and applications of safe, secure and trustworthy emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence;

(h)	Support the implementation of trade facilitation reforms, in particular the WTO Agreement on Trade Facilitation, as well as capacity-building for National Trade Facilitation Committees and bodies to increase the harmonization and coordination of trade facilitation measures at the national and regional levels, and continue its support for the development and implementation of appropriate legal and regulatory frameworks that reduce trade transaction costs;

(i)	Provide technical assistance, leveraging the flagship customs software and associated capacity-building of the Automated System for Customs Data to boost member States’ capacity to support sustainable economic transformation, collect revenue, protect society and secure borders;

(j)	Deliver technical guidance on data and policy recommendations and promote the sharing of best practices regarding the formulation and integration of strategic national policies, with the aim of integrating developing countries into regional and global supply and value chains and transport networks. This includes the flagship publication Review of Maritime Transport and other research products, advice on legislative frameworks and capacity-building, including the TrainForTrade programme on port management;

(k)	Continue to assist in designing and implementing policies and actions aimed at improving the efficiency of the management of transport operations, trade transactions and logistics, including through the digitalization and electronic exchange of trade-related documents, and support international and regional transport networks through policy dialogue and cooperation mechanisms, ensuring their sustainability and resilience, including the organization of the UNCTAD Global Supply Chain Forum;

(l)	Foster international policy dialogue on trade logistics issues, addressing the interests and challenges of developing countries in regional and international forums and focusing on specific vulnerabilities of landlocked developing countries and small island developing States, including through advocacy on transport, logistics and trade facilitation to enhance their resilience;
(m)	Support developing countries in sustainably harnessing the ocean economy and implementing international commitments on maritime transport and ocean governance;

(n)	Mainstream the cross-cutting issue of gender equality and the empowerment of women and girls across the three pillars of the subprogramme, with a special focus on empowering women digital entrepreneurs and enhancing their digital skills and opportunities.
12.64	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Improved capacity and policy formulation at the national, regional and global levels on e-commerce, digital trade and digital economy for sustainable and inclusive development;

(b)	Improved coordination among Governments, together with development partners, civil society and the private sector, to implement policies on e-commerce and the digital economy that work for sustainable and inclusive development;

(c)	Improved knowledge and understanding of policy options by policymakers and international consensus on emerging challenges and opportunities in science, technology and innovation for sustainable and inclusive development, as well as increased capacity of policymakers to assess, analyse and formulate policies on key issues on the international economic agenda;
(d)	Member States implementing the outcomes of the World Summit on the Information Society and of the Technology Facilitation Mechanism;
(e)	Member States harnessing science, technology and innovation for their national development strategies;

(f)	Countries addressing key and emerging issues in trade logistics, as well as regional and global supply chains, including maritime transport policies and port management and effective implementation of relevant international commitments on maritime transport and ocean governance;
(g)	Member States implementing trade facilitation reforms, including the WTO Agreement on Trade Facilitation;

(h)	Enhanced capacity of member States to design and implement policies and action aimed at improving the efficiency of trade transactions and of the management of transport operations, including through the Automated System for Customs Data programme.
Programme performance in 2025
Developing countries advance the conceptualization and development of science, technology and innovation parks for sustainable development

12.65	For developing countries, the ability to design and manage effective science, technology and innovation parks can help to foster innovation-led growth, stimulating local enterprise development and creating productive employment opportunities. To help to advance this, the subprogramme enhanced the capacity of policymakers and practitioners from four developing countries (Ghana, Mongolia, Mozambique and Uzbekistan) to design, operate and manage science, technology and innovation parks through a dedicated initiative aimed at strengthening policy and operational capacities and building expertise and practice. The subprogramme support included tailored capacity-building, peer learning and South-South knowledge exchange, and technical guidance and analysis such as a policy toolkit.

Result 3: strengthening inclusion in trade facilitation – Proposed programme plan for 2027

12.71	Harnessing inclusiveness is among the core Sustainable Development Goal objectives, while inclusive trade is a prerequisite for sustainable growth. Simplification, harmonization, standardization and digitalization of procedures and processes can yield outsized benefits for micro-, small and medium-sized enterprises, many of which are owned by women cross-border traders, as well as other people in vulnerable situations such as persons with disabilities. The subprogramme, through its Empowerment Programme for Trade Facilitation, has been assisting member States in devising policies, procedures, recommendations or workplans to ensure that cross-border trade is inclusive while reducing trade time and costs and improving supply chain efficiency.
Lessons learned and planned change

12.72	The lesson for the subprogramme was that assisting member States in the implementation of trade facilitation reforms also required a focused approach on inclusion in trade facilitation in terms of working with National Trade Facilitation Committees. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will support National Trade Facilitation Committees and government agencies in the implementation of targeted actions to devise policies procedures, recommendations or workplans for strengthening inclusion.

Subprogramme 5 – Africa, least developed countries and special programmes
Objective

12.75	The objective, to which this subprogramme contributes, is to enhance the effective integration of Africa, the least developed countries and other groups of countries in special situations (landlocked developing countries, small island developing States and other structurally weak, vulnerable and small economies) into the global economy through fostering sustainable structural economic transformation, reducing vulnerabilities and developing domestic productive capacities in the targeted groups.
Strategy
12.76	To contribute to the objective, the subprogramme will:

(a)	Carry out research and technical cooperation activities to diversify the exports, foster the productive capacities and explore the financing structures of the above-mentioned countries, including through contributions to the reports of the Secretary-General to the General Assembly and the reports on the implementation and follow-up of relevant agendas and programmes of action;

(b)	Implement technical cooperation programmes and develop strategic evidence- and policy-based options to promote sustainable structural economic transformation through productive capacity-building, diversification and support aimed at addressing vulnerability and building resilience, in particular for developing countries;

(c)	Advocate for consensus in the international development community regarding the policy measures that best address the sustainable development problems of least developed countries, small island developing States and landlocked developing countries, including at the multilateral level, through the identification of new policy approaches and methodologies, training and capacity-building workshops, including for parliamentarians, as well as greater interaction with research institutes in least developed countries and other developing countries, and with development partners;

(d)	Strengthen efforts to provide domestic policy support and capacity-building to the targeted groups derived from its innovative research and analysis, development of analytical tools and technical support;

(e)	Provide technical support to least developed countries that are either in the process of graduation or recently graduated from the least developed country category, and including through the provision of General Assembly-mandated vulnerability profiles, as applicable, to ensure timely support leading to their graduation with momentum and a smooth transition towards their new status;

(f)	Broaden its country-based technical support to include aspects such as national strategies for the development of productive capacities and structural economic transformation, inclusive trade, regional trade integration, rules of origin, inequality, poverty and vulnerability, enhanced market access and preference utilization, value addition in strategic products and geographical indications;

(g)	Support member States in addressing the socioeconomic impact of external shocks on economies in target countries, through research work and technical cooperation, in order to facilitate specific, data-driven policy design and implementation aimed at building resilience;

(h)	Support African countries in addressing their special concerns and needs, including as articulated in the New Partnership for Africa’s Development and in Agenda 2063: The Africa We Want, and in implementing the Agreement Establishing the African Continental Free Trade Area through research, technical cooperation and targeted policy advice;

(i)	Deliver training, capacity-building activities and ad hoc research on market access, the productive capacities index, trade preferences and rules of origin to support the WTO least developed countries group in identifying and negotiating, in line with the needs of the group, result-oriented common positions for the relevant WTO committee meetings.
12.77	The above-mentioned work is expected to result in:
(a)	Enhanced role of productive capacities in fostering structural economic transformation for inclusive growth and development in beneficiary countries;

(b)	The development and implementation by member States of evidence-based policies and programmes to deepen domestic and regional markets and accelerate industrialization and socioeconomic transformation;

(c)	Improved development policy formulation and implementation capacities in countries with specific needs, including the least developed countries and small island developing States, for enhanced resilience and structural transformations;
(d)	The adoption by graduating countries of policies and strategies, including to support a smooth transition out of the least developed country category;
(e)	Enhanced implementation of the African Continental Free Trade Area by member States;
(f)	Greater participation by member States in multilateral forums dedicated to enhanced trade through transparent, predictable and simple rules of origin;

(g)	Improved development policy formulation, implementation and monitoring capacities of member States, including greater policy coherence, and capacities to account for current and future shocks, reduce exposure to external shocks and build resilience;
(h)	Stronger representation of the interests of least developed countries in WTO policy discussions and ministerial meetings;
(i)	Progress by member States, in particular least developed countries and other groups of countries in special situations, towards the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals 8, 9, 10 and 17.
Programme performance in 2025
Least developed countries identify a common position on rules of origin and agenda for the Fourteenth Ministerial Conference of the World Trade Organization

12.78	The subprogramme supports the least developed countries group in WTO negotiations on rules of origin by providing technical assistance to help them to better understand WTO agreements and effectively articulate their interests in the multilateral trading system. Ahead of the Fourteenth Ministerial Conference of WTO, the subprogramme has expanded the scope of its training to effectively assist the least developed countries group in gaining a clearer understanding of the strategic issues shaping the negotiations and in identifying concrete pathways to enhance their use of preferential market access, and to help the group to formulate its position on the issue and introduce a proposal to be included in the outcome document.

children.text: Result 3: least developed countries enabled to formulate and implement inclusive and coherent trade policies – Proposed programme plan for 2027

12.84	International trade can support sustained growth in least developed countries, but its benefits can be uneven and may exacerbate inequality. The subprogramme supports least developed countries in identifying trade and other economic policies that are supportive of the goals of inequality and poverty reduction through policy-oriented research on the interlinkages between trade and poverty.
Lessons learned and planned change

12.85	The lesson for the subprogramme was that its trade-related assistance better supports poverty reduction when it explicitly integrates inclusion and addresses coherence with other economic and sectoral policies geared towards poverty eradication. In applying the lesson, the subprogramme will enhance the capacity of least developed countries to formulate and implement inclusive trade strategies that are coherent with macroeconomic and sectoral policies to foster poverty reduction through tailored analysis, policy options and capacity-building activities.
